title: The inter ocean curiosity shop for the year 1883
author: Various; Jones, William P. [Editor]

quoteFirst: The doctor turned himself in the bed, and said, 'God bless you, my dear!' These were the last words he spoke. His difficulty of breathing increased till
quoteTime: about seven o'clock in the evening
quoteLast: , when Mr. Barber and Mrs. Desmoulins, who were sitting in the room, observed that the noise he made in breathing had ceased, went to the bed, and found he was dead.
title: Stories of Authors, British and American
author: Chubb, Edwin Watts, 1865-1959

quoteLast: The bier, composed of two long pieces of bamboo, with a couple of cross-pieces, and covered with a rich white shawl, was upheld by the deceased's eldest brother and three of his most intimate friends and relatives.
title: Cremation of the Dead: Its History and Bearings Upon Public Health
author: Eassie, William, 1832-1888

quoteFirst: I, therefore, directed him by telegraph, “if he thought nothing could be done at present, to return on board with all hands until the wind changed;” for this alone, as far as I could see the state of the _Fury_, seemed to offer the smallest chance of clearing the shore, so as to enable us to proceed with our work, or to attempt hauling the ship off the ground.
quoteLast: Captain Hoppner returned to the _Hecla_, accompanied by all hands, except an officer with a party at the pumps, reporting to me that the _Fury_ had been forced aground by the ice pressing on the masses lying near her, and bringing home, if not breaking, the seaward anchor, so that the ship was soon found to have sewed from two to three feet fore and aft.
title: Journal of the Third Voyage for the Discovery of a North-West Passage
author: Parry, William Edward, Sir, 1790-1855; Morley, Henry, 1822-1894 [Editor]

quoteLast: the signal bell for landing on the rock was again rung, when every man was at his quarters. In this service it was thought more appropriate to use the bell than to _pipe_ to quarters, as the use of this instrument is less known to the mechanic than the sound of the bell.
title:
An Account of the Bell Rock Light-House
Including the Details of the Erection and Peculiar Structure of That Edifice; to Which Is Prefixed a Historical View of the Institution and Progress of the Northern Light-Houses
author: Stevenson, Robert, 1772-1850

quoteFirst: Harry was soon riding at a smart pace along the road to Bergen-op-Zoom, whence he made due south for Lillo, reaching that small fortified place
quoteTime: about seven o’clock in the evening
quoteLast: .  To his intense disappointment he found that General van Santen was at the British head-quarters at Thielen.  He had been absent all day, but was expected to return before night.
title: The Adventures of Harry Rochester: A Tale of the Days of Marlborough and Eugene
author: Strang, Herbert; Rainey, W. (William), 1852-1936 [Illustrator]

quoteFirst: As it gave the name and address of the writer, I went, and again it was Christmas Eve. The snow lay deep on the ground, and a hard frost had bound it together; the fog was choking and almost impenetrable, when
quoteLast: I found myself knocking at the door of the house. There was no light in the passage, but someone--I could not see who--answered the door.
title: Pictures and Problems from London Police Courts
author: Holmes, Thomas, 1846-1918

quoteFirst: Beside his own practical knowledge of the business committed to his charge in minutiæ, he employs only men trained from their youth up in the art of plate engraving, to perform the skilled labor, or to superintend the divisions of this most important Governmental Bureau. The responsibilities and mental anxieties of its chief are so inexorable, that he must be at his post by a little past seven in the morning, and remain till five P. M. He must return
quoteLast: , and remain until ten at night. Often the wheels and presses, and patient hands of this department, go from day to day to be able to meet the enormous demand of the country upon its resources.
title:
Ten Years in Washington
or, Inside Life and Scenes in Our National Capital as a Woman Sees Them ... to Which Is Added a Full Account of the Life and Death of President James A. Garfield
author: Clemmer, Mary, 1839-1884; Shipley, J. L. [Contributor]

quoteLast: The thermometer at -33° and falling; barometer, aneroid, 30°. 74; attached thermometer, 86°. Wind steady, W.N.W. The meteor resembled an illuminated cloud; illuminated, because seen against the deep blue night sky; otherwise it resembled the mackerel fleeces and mare's tails of our summer skies at home.
title: Adrift in the Arctic ice pack
author: Kane, Elisha Kent, 1820-1857; Kephart, Horace, 1862-1931 [Editor]

quoteLast: we branched off from the main path, and descended on foot a steep path into a thickly wooded valley. In a clearing of the trees stood a collection of wooden huts, a summer village of shepherds, called Raskrsnica.
title: The Land of the Black Mountain: The Adventures of Two Englishmen in Montenegro
author: Wyon, Reginald, 1872-1921; Prance, Gerald
